title: Choosing a Learning Rate Schedule
concept: Choosing a learning rate schedule means matching the training pattern to the tool: fixed for simple baselines, step decay for planned drops, cosine decay for smooth long runs, and warmup for unstable starts.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is warmup mainly trying to protect against?

options.text: Unstable or explosive updates at the very beginning of training.
Having too many lesson cards in a segment.

exercises.explain: Right. Warmup is a beginning-of-training stabilizer.

cards.title: Different roads, different gears
cards.body: A short city trip, a mountain climb, and a highway cruise do not call for the same driving strategy. Learning rate schedules are similar: the right choice depends on whether you need simplicity, planned drops, a smooth finish, or a safer launch.

cards.title: Pick the schedule that matches the training story
cards.body: A schedule choice is really a question of when and how you want the learning rate to change. Fixed means no schedule at all. Step decay lowers the rate at planned milestones. Cosine decay lets it fade smoothly. Warmup protects the opening steps and is often paired with one of the other options afterward.

cards.title: Choose the shoe for the terrain
cards.body: Running spikes are great for a track but awkward on rocky trails. Hiking boots are great on rocks but clumsy for a sprint. In the same way, a schedule is not globally best — it is best for a certain training surface.

cards.title: A quick decision checklist
cards.body: For a short baseline or a simple experiment, a fixed rate may be enough. If you want obvious gear shifts at known times, use step decay. If you want a long smooth glide into fine-tuning, try cosine decay. If the first batches are unstable, add warmup at the front.

cards.title: There is no single best schedule for every model
cards.body: A common myth is that one famous schedule should win on every problem. But schedule choice depends on the training pattern you see. The smarter move is to match the tool to the symptom instead of treating every run the same.

cards.title: Simple defaults vs. stability helpers
cards.body: Some choices are about how the whole run should slow down. Warmup is about making the start safer and can be combined with those choices.

left.text:
Fixed for simple baselines.
Step decay for planned drops.
Cosine decay for a smooth long fade.

right.text:
Warmup for fragile starts.
Often added before step or cosine.
Protects the opening phase.

exercises.prompt: You are running a short, simple baseline just to get a reference result. Which choice is often the most reasonable place to start?

options.text: Warmup plus cosine plus random jumps every batch.
A schedule chosen only by emoji.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A fixed rate is a useful baseline when the run is simple and you mainly want a clean starting point.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Warmup can be combined with another schedule, such as step decay or cosine decay, instead of standing alone for the entire run.
exercises.explain: True. Warmup usually handles the start, then another schedule takes over for the main part of training.

exercises.sentence: If training is unstable only at the beginning, add ___.
exercises.explain: Correct. Warmup specifically targets unstable opening steps.

exercises.prompt: Which plan sounds best matched to the problem?

options.text: Use a fixed rate for a quick baseline, step decay for clear milestone drops, cosine decay for a smooth long run, and warmup when the start is fragile.
Always use the exact same schedule for every model, dataset, and training pattern.
Choose schedules only by whichever one has the fanciest name.

exercises.explain: Right. Good schedule choice is about matching the training story to the tool.

exercises.prompt: You are training a deep vision model for a long run. The first few hundred steps are unstable, and after that you want the learning rate to shrink smoothly without hand-picking many milestone epochs. Which plan fits best?

options.text: Use warmup at the start, then switch into cosine decay for the long smooth fade.
Use one fixed learning rate forever and ignore the unstable beginning.
Use step decay only because warmup can never be combined with anything else.
Remove the optimizer after the first epoch so the learning rate no longer matters.

exercises.explain: Excellent. Warmup handles the fragile start, and cosine decay handles the smooth long finish.
